does: This paper introduces Token-level Direct Preference Optimization (TDPO), which extends direct preference optimization to operate at the token level rather than on complete sequences. The method applies the Bradley-Terry model to construct token-based reward signals from pairwise preference annotations, then optimizes policy with forward KL divergence constraints computed per-token to prevent excessive divergence from the reference model. Unlike sequence-level approaches that evaluate full generated responses holistically, TDPO leverages the autoregressive structure of language generation by applying preference optimization to individual token decisions, thereby improving both alignment with human preferences and output diversity without requiring explicit reward model training.
connects: This work builds on the direct preference optimization (DPO) framework, which itself emerged as a simpler alternative to reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) by directly optimizing preferences without a separate reward model. TDPO responds to limitations in how DPO and related methods handle KL divergence control, which typically operates at the sequence level and can lead to inefficient divergence management. The paper connects to broader research on preference-based alignment methods and the tension between KL regularization strength and generation diversity. It also draws on the classical Bradley-Terry model from ranking and preference learning, adapting it to the token-level setting where sequential decisions compound to form full outputs.
enables: By moving optimization to the token level with explicit per-token KL constraints, TDPO opens pathways for more fine-grained control over model behavior during generation. This approach could enable better understanding of where alignment failures occur in sequences and allow targeted interventions at critical decision points. The method potentially allows for more efficient use of preference data by extracting signal from every token position rather than only aggregate comparisons. Future work could extend this framework to hierarchical preference optimization spanning multiple granularities, apply it to other structured prediction tasks beyond text, or combine token-level constraints with other alignment techniques like constitutional AI for more nuanced control over model outputs.
